title: Whoï؟½ll Wear The Beaten Colours?
content:
آ آ آ آ Whoï؟½ll wear the beaten colours, and cheer the beaten men?
آ آ آ آ Whoï؟½ll wear the beaten colours, till our time comes again?
آ آ آ آ Where sullen crowds are densest, and fickle as the sea,
آ آ آ آ Whoï؟½ll wear the beaten colours, and wear them home with me?

آ آ آ آ We closed the bars and gambling dens and voted straight and clean,
آ آ آ آ Our women walked while motor cars were whirling round the scene,
آ آ آ آ The Potts Point Vote was one for Greed and Ease and Luxury
آ آ آ آ With all to hold, and coward gold, and beaten folk are we.

آ آ آ آ Whoï؟½ll wear the beaten colours, with hands and pockets clean?
آ آ آ آ (I wore the beaten colours since I was seventeen)
آ آ آ آ I wore them up, and wore them down, Outback and across the sea,
آ آ آ آ Whoï؟½ll wear the beaten colours, and wear them home with me?

آ آ آ آ We wore them back from Ladysmith to where the peace was signed,
آ آ آ آ And wore them through the London streets where Jingoes howled behind.
آ آ آ آ We wore them to the Queenï؟½s Hall, while England yelled ï؟½Pro-Boers!ï؟½
آ آ آ آ And sat them over victory while London banged the doors.1

آ آ آ آ We wore them from Port Arthur round till all sunk in the sea,
آ آ آ آ (Whoï؟½ll wear the white manï؟½s colours, and wear them home with me?)
آ آ آ آ Iï؟½ve worn them through with gentlemen, with work-slaves and alone,
آ آ آ آ Whoï؟½ll wear the beaten colours, boys, and wear them on his own?

آ آ آ آ Thereï؟½s one would look with startled eyes and shrink while I caressed,
آ آ آ آ Came I not with the colours of the conquered on my breast.
آ آ آ آ And twenty thousand Bushmen would stand with hands behind
آ آ آ آ And scorn in all their faces for the coward of his kind.

آ آ آ آ Whoï؟½ll wear the beaten colours and raise the voice they drowned,
آ آ آ آ It may be when we march again, theyï؟½ll bear some other sound,
آ آ آ آ Whoï؟½ll pin the beaten colours on and drive the beaten pen,
آ آ آ آ It may be other steel and ink when we march out again.
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
